statement: Food and non-alcoholic beverage prices in Iceland are approximately 50–70% higher than the EU-27 average, according to Eurostat comparative price level indices (2024). This makes Iceland one of the most expensive countries in Europe for food.
caveats: High food prices reflect multiple factors: transport costs, small market, agricultural protection, import tariffs, and VAT. EU membership alone would not eliminate all price differences — Norway, also outside the EU's CAP, has similarly high food prices.
